_meta.purpose: AGRIVEDA Smart Crop Advisory - base data: which crops are agronomically grown where, by season
_meta.how_to_use: Match farmer's selected State (and District, if UP) to the Kharif/Rabi/Zaid crop lists. This is the 'what CAN grow here' layer only.
_meta.important_for_profit_feature: Screen shows 'sabse zyada munafa kis mein hai' - THIS file only tells you which crops are viable in a region. To actually rank by profit you need a second dataset layered on top: cost-of-cultivation per acre, expected yield per acre, and current mandi price per crop per region. Same crop list, different multiplier per district.

seasons.kharif: Sown Jun-Jul with monsoon onset, harvested Sep-Oct
seasons.rabi: Sown Oct-Dec after monsoon retreat, harvested Feb-Apr, mostly irrigation/soil-moisture dependent
seasons.zaid: Short summer crop, sown Mar, harvested Jun, needs assured irrigation since there's no rain support

_meta.coverage: All India states/UTs at state level (~30 entries). Uttar Pradesh additionally broken into its 9 official agro-climatic zones covering all 75 districts, since that's AGRIVEDA's core operating region.
_meta.why_zones_not_raw_districts: Crop choice follows soil/rainfall/temperature, not political boundaries - neighbouring districts in the same agro-climatic zone have almost identical crop calendars (ICAR and state agri departments plan this way too). A flat district-by-district list nationwide would mostly repeat the same handful of entries per zone while being far harder to keep accurate and maintain. Aligarh and Bulandshahr, for example, sit in two DIFFERENT UP zones despite being neighbours - which is exactly the kind of distinction a flat 'Western UP' label would miss.

uttar_pradesh_zones.note: Higher rainfall (100-150cm), forest-adjacent, shallow water table - favours paddy and sugarcane
Alluvial sandy loam, canal + tubewell irrigated, mixed rice-sugarcane belt
Highest irrigation intensity in UP, classic sugarcane-wheat belt, close to NCR vegetable market
Lower, less reliable rainfall (~75cm) - drought-tolerant Kharif crops outperform water-hungry paddy here
Core Ganga-Yamuna doab, classic rice-wheat rotation, highest cropping intensity in the state

zaid: Very limited - low irrigation cover (~25% vs 60% state average), most land left fallow
uttar_pradesh_zones.note: Drought-prone plateau, red-black rocky soil (local names: Rakar, Parwa, Kabar, Maar) - chana is the standout Rabi crop
High rainfall (100-130cm), flood-prone in monsoon, sandy alluvial soil - paddy and sugarcane dominate
Dense rice-wheat belt along the Ganga; Pratapgarh/Amethi belt also notable for aonla and mentha (menthol mint)
Plateau-like terrain, red/stony/lateritic soil, ~40% forest cover - coarse cereals and pulses outperform paddy/wheat

rabi: None - single short summer growing season only

zaid: Limited - apple/stone-fruit horticulture dominates farm income instead

india_states.note: Semi-arid state - crop choice varies sharply between canal-irrigated (Sriganganagar/Hanumangarh) and rainfed tracts
India's largest soybean and gram (chana) producer
Traditional rice bowl - Kharif paddy dominates; Rabi area stays small since most land is rainfed
One of the few states where Rabi jowar (not just Kharif) is a major crop, especially in Marathwada
Coconut, cashew and areca (perennial crops) matter more here than seasonal cropping
Local season names (Kuruvai/Samba/Thaladi/Navarai) map roughly to Kharif/Rabi/Zaid but follow their own calendar
Perennial plantation crops - coconut, rubber, pepper, cardamom, banana - matter far more than the Kharif/Rabi/Zaid cycle here
Grows 3 paddy crops a year (Aus-Aman-Boro) - unusual nationally
Mostly rainfed - Rabi area is much smaller than Kharif due to limited irrigation
One of the few states where maize is a major crop in all three seasons (Kosi/Purnea belt especially)
Tea is the dominant cash crop but it's a perennial plantation crop, not a seasonal one

india_states.state: North-Eastern Hill States (Meghalaya, Manipur, Mizoram, Nagaland, Tripura, Sikkim, Arunachal Pradesh)
india_states.note: Jhum (shifting) cultivation common in hills; ginger, turmeric, orange and pineapple are important cash crops alongside grain

india_states.state: Delhi / Chandigarh / Puducherry / Andaman & Nicobar / Lakshadweep
india_states.note: Minimal or highly localised agriculture (urban-adjacent vegetables, horticulture, or island cropping) - not broken out in detail here
